one_line_takeaway: Cliopatra shows that Clio-style LLM-based chat-insight pipelines (PII redaction + clustering + summarization + LLM auditing) can be poisoned with nested prompt injections to leak a target user's private medical information into public cluster summaries with up to 100% precision, while Clio's own LLM-based privacy auditor catches none of the successful leaks (0/163 clusters flagged), whereas a formal differential-privacy alternative (URANIA) meaningfully reduces attack precision at tight privacy budgets.
key_finding: Facet extraction leaks PII despite explicit redaction instructions: across model families, age was leaked in 2-44% of chats, gender in 53-63%, symptoms in 27-92%, and disease in 39-73% (Table 2). With only one known symptom, poisons correctly clustered with the target chat in 89% (Qwen), 46% (Gemma), 52% (LLaMA), and 43% (Claude) of cases; the Regex Attack then extracted the disease in up to 71% of chats (Qwen) versus a 22% baseline (a 2x improvement), and the LLM Attack further improved this to 81% for Qwen (Table 3). The Confident Regex (C-Regex) variant achieved 100% precision for Gemma, LLaMA, and Claude configurations (98.4% for Qwen) at abstention rates of 80-83% (Table 4), i.e., when the attack commits to an answer it is almost always correct. Fast Clio reproduced Full Clio's attack success rate exactly across all four model families (both 71/37/37/34%), validating it as a cheaper evaluation proxy (Table 5). The stealthy variant cut overt PII in cluster summaries from ~100% to 0-15% while retaining 100% precision, at the cost of higher abstention (Table 6). Scaling to 100K chats increased abstention (e.g., Claude family: 74% -> 92% -> 98% at 1K/10K/100K) but the attack still extracted the disease from 2% of chats at 100K with 100% precision versus a 53.2% baseline precision (Section 4.4). Increasing known symptoms from 1 to 5 monotonically raised attack success toward ~100% for Qwen. Clio's own LLM-based privacy auditor failed to catch any of 163 successfully-extracted-disease clusters across all four model families and auditor choices (0/163 flagged with rating <=2), and 57.7% of successful-attack clusters were rated a 'perfect' 5/5 (Table 8). By contrast, URANIA's differential-privacy mechanism substantially reduced attack precision at epsilon=25 (15-51% versus Clio's 100%) though it degraded to 58-100% at the looser epsilon=50 (Table 9), and the paper notes URANIA provides record-level rather than user-level DP and incurs utility degradation reported by its original authors.
core_contribution: The paper presents Cliopatra, the first end-to-end privacy attack against 'privacy-preserving' LLM-based chat-insights systems (systems that chain multiple LMs to redact PII, cluster chats, summarize clusters, and audit privacy, e.g., Anthropic's Clio). The attack nests prompt injections inside crafted 'poison' chats so that a single adversary who inserts C-1 poison chats (C = minimum cluster size) simultaneously (1) survives PII redaction during facet extraction, (2) clusters with a target user's chat via embedding proximity, (3) coerces the cluster summarizer into emitting the target's private information in the public cluster summary, and (4) evades both automated LLM-based privacy auditors and (via a stealthy variant) human inspection by disguising the leaked summary as an innocuous statement (e.g., 'write a paper about link between covid-19 and disease X'). The authors also build a lower-cost evaluation harness ('Fast Clio') that only summarizes the poisoned cluster and show it reproduces Full Clio's attack success exactly, and they test the attack against URANIA, a differential-privacy-based alternative to Clio, as a mitigation comparison.
threat_model: External adversary who can create a small number of accounts to insert chats into the target system (reasonable since these platforms ingest public-facing chats and automated accounts are known to interact with AI assistants); knows internal system parameters such as which LLMs are used and the minimum cluster size C (a 'standard security principles' / Kerckhoffs-style assumption, since these systems typically use publicly documented models); has partial knowledge of the target user (age, gender, and a subset of symptoms, obtainable publicly or from an acquaintance) plus, for evaluation convenience, the closed list of candidate diseases; and has access only to the system's public outputs (published cluster summaries/insights), not to raw chats or internal system state. The paper explicitly frames this as combining data poisoning, prompt injection, and attribute inference into a single attack chain and notes prompt injection is an understudied risk specifically for LLM-based insight systems (as opposed to RAG systems, which the authors argue are simpler and lack the multi-stage anonymization pipeline Clio has).
limitations: Target chats and personas are synthetic (generated via Claude Sonnet 4.5 from NLICE dataset attributes), not real user data, for ethical reasons -- the paper acknowledges this and argues synthetic chats closely resemble real WildChat conversations but does not quantify this resemblance beyond a qualitative appendix claim. The adversary's knowledge of the closed candidate-disease list is an evaluation simplification the authors flag as unrealistic ('for ease of evaluation'). Clio's originally published configuration (Haiku 3 + Sonnet 3.5) could not be exactly reproduced because Sonnet 3.5 has been deprecated, so results are reported for reconfigured model pairings rather than the exact system as originally deployed and evaluated by its creators. The paper assumes single-conversation-per-user contribution when discussing URANIA's DP guarantees, which the authors note may not hold in practice and could weaken the comparison. Results are demonstrated on one domain (medical target chats); generality to other sensitive domains is asserted (with an appendix pointer) but not empirically evaluated in the body of the paper. Evaluation is limited to Clio (the only well-documented system available) and URANIA as a DP alternative; other deployed 'privacy-preserving' insight systems referenced in the introduction were not evaluated because their prompts/implementation details are undisclosed.

datasets_used: WildChat (1M real AI-assistant conversations; sampled at 1K/10K/100K chats)
NLICE synthetic patient-record dataset (47 diseases, used to sample age/gender/symptom/disease personas)
Synthetically generated medical target chats (100 chats, generated by prompting Claude Sonnet 4.5 with sampled personas)
quantitative_results: Facet leakage by info type across model families (Table 2): age 2-44%, gender 53-63%, symptom(s) 27-92%, disease 39-73%
Poison-target clustering rate with 1 known symptom (Table 3): 89% Qwen, 46% Gemma, 52% LLaMA, 43% Claude
Attack success rate (Regex Attack, 1 symptom, Table 3): 71% Qwen, 37% Gemma, 37% LLaMA, 34% Claude vs 22% baseline
Attack success rate (LLM Attack, 1 symptom, Table 3): 81% Qwen, 44% Gemma, 42% LLaMA, 39% Claude
C-Regex precision/abstention (Table 4): 98.4%/35% Qwen, 100.0%/81% Gemma, 100.0%/80% LLaMA, 100.0%/83% Claude, vs 22% baseline precision
Full vs Fast Clio attack success (Table 5): identical, 71%/37%/37%/34% for Qwen/Gemma/LLaMA/Claude
Stealthy attack PII containment (Table 6): reduced from ~100% (non-stealthy, all models) to 1.08% Qwen, 13.3% Gemma, 0% LLaMA, 14.6% Claude, while maintaining 100% precision
Stealthy vs non-stealthy abstention rate (Table 6): Qwen 23%->30%, Gemma 63%->87%, LLaMA 77%->56%, Claude 74%->75%
Scaling abstention (Claude family, Section 4.4): 74% (1K) -> 92% (10K) -> 98% (100K); at 100K, 2% of chats still confidently attacked at 100% precision vs 53.2% baseline precision

Privacy auditor efficacy (Table 8): 0 of 163 successfully-attacked clusters (across 4 model families and 2 auditor choices, 'Same' and Claude Sonnet 4.5) flagged with privacy rating <=2; 57.7% rated 5/5
URANIA vs Clio precision (Table 9): epsilon=25 gives 51% Qwen/15% Gemma/35% LLaMA/48% Claude; epsilon=50 gives 100%/71%/58%/65%; Clio (no DP) gives 100% across all four
baselines_compared: Public-information-only baseline (Claude Sonnet 4.5 guesses disease from age/gender/known symptoms/candidate list, no Clio output access)
Regex Attack vs LLM Attack (two extraction strategies for Cliopatra)
Full Clio vs Fast Clio (evaluation-cost reduction, shown to be equivalent)
Clio's ad hoc LLM-based privacy auditor (same-model and Claude Sonnet 4.5 auditor) as a mitigation
URANIA differential-privacy system at epsilon=25 and epsilon=50 as a formal-guarantee mitigation, compared against undefended Clio
